item.title: Palazzo da Mula, Venice
item.museum: National Gallery of Art, Washington, D.C.
item.lesson: Monet resisted Venice for decades. Too beautiful to be painted, he said — too painted already, by everyone we have met this week — and it took a friend's invitation, a palazzo guest room, and his wife Alice's enthusiasm to get the sixty-eight-year-old there at last, in the autumn of 1908. He never painted Venice's views. He painted its condition. Here a Gothic palace's facade is taken head-on, cropped of sky and pavement, so that the entire canvas becomes what Venice actually is: a wall of worked stone standing in water, each dissolving into the other's color. The palace is blue the way embers are red — saturated from inside. The water repeats the architecture in loosening bands until masonry becomes current. Alice wrote home that he worked in a fury, six canvases a day in shifts timed to the light, muttering that he had arrived too late in his life. Ten mornings into our question, Monet's first answer: stop separating the city from the water. Venice is the reflection.
item.lookFor: Notice there is no horizon, no sky, no ground — nothing to stand on anywhere. Monet removes every surface except the two that interested him: stone and water, trading their colors.
